source_intake_record.raw_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $\angle ACB = 90^\circ$. Point D is on the extension line of AB, and CD is connected. If $AB = 2BD$ and $\tan\angle BCD = \frac{2}{3}$, what is the value of $\frac{AC}{BC}$?
source_intake_record.raw_answer_text:
\textbf{Solution:} Draw $DM\perp BC$ from point D, intersecting the extension of BC at point M,\\
$\because$ $\angle ACB=\angle DMB=90^\circ$, $\angle ABC=\angle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle ABC\sim \triangle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\because$ AB＝2BD,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}=\frac{1}{2}$,\\
In $\triangle CDM$,\\
since $\tan\angle MCD=\frac{2}{3}=\frac{DM}{CM}$, let DM＝2k, then CM＝3k,\\
also $\because$ $\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\therefore$ BC＝2k, AC＝4k,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{AC}{BC}=\frac{4k}{2k}=\boxed{2}$.

image_dependency: question_text starts with "As shown in the figure," indicating explicit figure grounding
requires_image=true and text_dominant=false both point to meaningful visual dependence
the problem is geometry with named points, extension lines, and an angle defined at interior/exterior points, which commonly relies on diagram interpretation
multi_step: the provided answer uses an auxiliary construction (draw DM perpendicular to BC) before solving
the solution combines triangle similarity, proportional reasoning from AB=2BD, and a tangent relation tan(angle BCD)=2/3
the target quantity AC/BC is not read directly and requires chaining multiple derived ratios
verifiability: the final target is a single numeric ratio, and the provided answer concludes with a concrete value 2
the reasoning uses standard geometric identities and ratio checks that are in principle rule-checkable
asset_registry_record shows question_present=true, answer_present=true, and registry_passed=true, so the sample is structurally complete

normalization_record.normalized_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $\angle ACB = 90^\circ$. Point D is on the extension line of AB, and CD is connected. If $AB = 2BD$ and $\tan\angle BCD = \frac{2}{3}$, what is the value of $\frac{AC}{BC}$?
normalization_record.normalized_answer_text:
Solution: Draw $DM\perp BC$ from point D, intersecting the extension of BC at point M,\\
$\because$ $\angle ACB=\angle DMB=90^\circ$, $\angle ABC=\angle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle ABC\sim \triangle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\because$ $AB=2BD$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}=\frac{1}{2}$,\\
In $\triangle CDM$,\\
since $\tan\angle MCD=\frac{2}{3}=\frac{DM}{CM}$, let $DM=2k$, then $CM=3k$,\\
also $\because$ $\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\therefore$ $BC=2k$, $AC=4k$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{AC}{BC}=\frac{4k}{2k}=\boxed{2}$
normalization_notes: Removed formatting-only markup from the answer and normalized equation symbols.
Question explicitly references a figure, and force_requires_image=true, so image dependence is preserved.

since $\tan\angle MCD=\frac{2}{3}=\frac{DM}{CM}$, let $DM=2k$, then $CM=3k$,\\
also $\because$ $\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\therefore$ $BC=2k$, $AC=4k$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{AC}{BC}=\frac{4k}{2k}=\boxed{2}$

open_ended_problem_variants.title: MM-Math 开放题
open_ended_problem_variants.rewritten_question_text: As shown in the figure, in $\triangle ABC$, $\angle ACB = 90^\circ$. Point D is on the extension line of AB, and CD is connected. If $AB = 2BD$ and $\tan\angle BCD = \frac{2}{3}$, what is the value of $\frac{AC}{BC}$?
open_ended_problem_variants.expected_answer:
Solution: Draw $DM\perp BC$ from point D, intersecting the extension of BC at point M,\\
$\because$ $\angle ACB=\angle DMB=90^\circ$, $\angle ABC=\angle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\triangle ABC\sim \triangle DBM$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\because$ $AB=2BD$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{BD}{AB}=\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{DM}{AC}=\frac{1}{2}$,\\
In $\triangle CDM$,\\
since $\tan\angle MCD=\frac{2}{3}=\frac{DM}{CM}$, let $DM=2k$, then $CM=3k$,\\
also $\because$ $\frac{BM}{BC}=\frac{1}{2}=\frac{DM}{AC}$,\\
$\therefore$ $BC=2k$, $AC=4k$,\\
$\therefore$ $\frac{AC}{BC}=\frac{4k}{2k}=\boxed{2}$
